title: Q: When should I use RLS vs OLS in Power BI?
summary: RLS filters rows based on user identity; OLS hides entire columns. Use RLS for tenant/region/role isolation, OLS for column-level secrets like SSN or salary.
content:
Q: When should I use RLS vs OLS in Power BI?

A:

Row-Level Security (RLS) filters which rows a user can see. Use it when:

  • Multi-tenant scenarios (each client sees only their rows).
  • Regional/territorial scoping (sales reps see only their territory).
  • Hierarchy-based access (managers see their reports' data).

Object-Level Security (OLS) hides entire tables or columns. Use it when:

  • Sensitive columns must be invisible (SSN, salary, margin).
  • Some users should not even know a table exists.
  • Compliance requires column-level redaction in reports.

Combining: RLS + OLS together is supported and common. Audit relationships and bidirectional cross-filters before assuming the model is locked down -- a single bidirectional relationship can leak rows across an RLS boundary.

P3 tip: always test with 'View as Role' for RLS and a non-admin test account for OLS (admin sees everything regardless of OLS).
author: P3 IQ Practice

title: Q: Should P3 build a Custom Engine Agent or a Cowork plugin?
summary: Both. Custom Engine Agent (CEA) gives P3 deep control of the model + retrieval stack; Cowork plugin is the right surface for ambient grounding inside M365.
content:
Q: Should P3 build a Custom Engine Agent or a Cowork plugin?

A:

The two are complementary, not exclusive.

Custom Engine Agent (CEA) -- e.g., p3-iq-cea -- gives P3 full control of the model (Kimi K2.6 today), retrieval stack (Foundry IQ + 17 Microsoft sources), and orchestration. CEAs surface in Teams via M365Extensions channel and in LibreChat via /v1/chat/completions. Use when: P3 needs deterministic behavior, custom tool chains, audit logging, or bring-your-own-model economics.

Cowork plugin / Graph Connector gives P3 distribution inside the M365 chrome (Copilot in Word, Outlook, Teams) without building UX. Cowork handles conversation; P3 surfaces enrichment via this Graph Connector + Skills + declarative agent manifests. Use when: P3 wants ambient presence wherever the user already works, and is OK with Cowork's Anthropic-powered orchestration.

P3 strategy: ship both. CEA for the dedicated 'P3 IQ' workflow; Graph Connector + Skills for ambient discovery via Cowork.
author: P3 IQ Practice

title: Q: Bridge mode or OBO for new MCP integrations?
summary: Default to bridge mode (3 headers) for inter-P3-service trust. Use OBO only when the downstream API requires audience-specific delegated tokens that LibreChat must mint per-call.
content:
Q: Bridge mode or OBO for new MCP integrations?

A:

Default: bridge mode. For any new MCP that lives inside the P3 platform and needs to act as a user, start with bridge mode: X-Bridge-Token (shared secret), X-Microsoft-Token (forwarded delegated AAD token), X-User-Email (audit). It's simpler, faster, and battle-tested across p3-iq-mcp-gateway, p3-harvest-mcp, etc.

Use OBO when: the downstream API requires audience-specific tokens minted with LibreChat's app credentials (e.g., calling Graph with a Files.Read.All-scoped token that wasn't in the original user's consent). Wire preProcessXTokens in graph.ts + {{LIBRECHAT_X_ACCESS_TOKEN}} placeholder in librechat-staging.yaml.

NEVER: SSE transport with per-user OBO. SSE processes tool calls in the GET task's context, so per-POST headers don't reach the MCP principal. Always type: streamable-http for delegated-token MCPs.
author: P3 IQ Practice
